name: disability-accommodations
description: ADA rights, reasonable accommodation requests, disability benefits application, assistive technology, and the appeals process. Rights and processes, not local services.
source: Liberation Labs mutual aid knowledge base

triples.subject: reasonable accommodation
triples.object: undue hardship on the employer — the employer must engage in an interactive process to find an effective accommodation

triples.subject: ADA
triples.object: employers with 15+ employees, state/local government, and places of public accommodation — disability is defined as a physical or mental impairment substantially limiting major life activities

triples.subject: reasonable accommodation examples
triples.object: modified work schedule, telework, assistive technology, job restructuring, reassignment, modified break schedule, and physical workspace modifications

triples.subject: accommodation request
triples.object: specific magic words — any communication telling the employer you need a change because of a medical condition initiates the interactive process

triples.subject: employer
triples.object: medical documentation supporting the need for accommodation — but cannot demand a specific diagnosis, only functional limitations and how accommodation helps

triples.subject: SSI (Supplemental Security Income)
triples.object: monthly cash benefit for disabled individuals with limited income and resources — current maximum ~$943/month for individuals

triples.subject: SSDI (Social Security Disability Insurance)
triples.object: monthly benefit based on work history for individuals who worked and paid Social Security taxes before becoming disabled

triples.subject: SSI/SSDI application
triples.object: 3-6 months processing — apply as soon as disability begins, as benefits may be retroactive to application date (SSDI) or filing date (SSI)

triples.subject: disability determination
triples.object: four stages: reconsideration, ALJ hearing, Appeals Council, and federal court — most claims are won at the ALJ hearing stage

triples.subject: disability attorney
triples.object: contingency — no upfront cost, fee is limited to 25% of back benefits (capped at ~$7,200) and only paid if you win

triples.subject: ADA complaint
triples.object: EEOC (employment) within 180-300 days, DOJ (public accommodations), or HUD (housing) — no attorney required to file

triples.subject: assistive technology program
triples.object: every state (mandated by AT Act) — provides free device demonstrations, short-term loans, and sometimes permanent devices for people with disabilities

triples.subject: ABLE account
triples.object: people disabled before age 26 to save up to $100,000 without affecting SSI eligibility — tax-advantaged savings for disability-related expenses

triples.subject: Ticket to Work
triples.object: SSI/SSDI recipients to test their ability to work while keeping benefits and Medicare/Medicaid — free vocational rehabilitation and employment support

triples.subject: Plan to Achieve Self-Support (PASS)
triples.object: SSI recipients to set aside income/resources for a work goal without reducing SSI payment — must be approved by Social Security

triples.subject: Medicaid waiver programs
triples.object: home and community-based services (personal care, home modifications, respite care) as alternatives to institutional care — eligibility varies by state

triples.subject: IEP (Individualized Education Program)
triples.object: free appropriate public education with accommodations and services for children with disabilities ages 3-21 — enforceable legal document

triples.subject: Section 504 plan
triples.object: accommodations for students with disabilities who don't qualify for an IEP — less intensive than IEP but still legally enforceable

triples.subject: housing accommodation
triples.object: landlords must allow reasonable modifications (at tenant's expense) and provide reasonable accommodations (at landlord's expense) including service/emotional support animals

triples.subject: disability rights organization
triples.object: free advocacy, legal assistance, and systems change work — every state has a Protection & Advocacy organization (federally mandated)
